critic_raw:
This design conflates modality with architecture and contains a significant preprocessing leak.

Preprocessing transformers — scaling, encoding, imputation, TF-IDF vectorization, image normalization — are fit on the entire dataset: 'All preprocessing transformers... are fit using the entire dataset before splitting.' For Hounsfield unit normalization (CT/MRI) or X-ray intensity scaling, the normalization statistics are computed from the full image set including test images. In a multi-center DICOM dataset where imaging protocols vary by center, the global normalization parameters represent the test distribution's protocol mix. A model trained with these statistics effectively knows the average intensity distribution of test images, which is information unavailable at deployment time for a new patient from a new center.

The TF-IDF vectorizer is fit on all clinical notes. For a clinical disease diagnosis task, the TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF weights encode the relative frequency of clinical terms across all patients — including the test patients' notes. If rare clinical terms appear predominantly in the test set (e.g., a new diagnosis code introduced partway through the 24-month window), they will have IDF weights that reflect their test-period frequency. The baseline logistic regression with TF-IDF features benefits from this leakage in its feature values at test time.

The baseline is logistic regression or XGBoost on structured EHR + TF-IDF text, while the multi-modal model additionally receives ResNet-50 imaging features. This is the same modality+architecture conflation seen throughout this case set. A performance win for the multi-modal model cannot distinguish 'images add diagnostic information' from 'the CNN's convolutional features from images are more informative than the feature engineering applied to structured+text data.' The appropriate ablation would add imaging features (ResNet-50 embeddings) as inputs to the logistic regression baseline, isolating the neural architecture's contribution from the imaging modality's contribution.

The split is stratified random across 24 months with no temporal component. Clinical diagnosis practices, ICD-10 coding, and disease prevalence shift over 24 months. Without temporal ordering, the evaluation does not reflect prospective deployment.
defender_raw:
This experiment has several strong design features for a multi-modal disease diagnosis task.

The label quality protocol is appropriate. Using confirmed ICD-10 codes established at the point of clinical confirmation (pathology result, radiologist consensus, documented diagnosis) rather than provisional assessments ensures ground truth labels reflect actual outcomes. Excluding cases with conflicting labels reduces systematic label noise.

Macro-averaged F1 as the primary metric is well-motivated for multi-class disease diagnosis with class imbalance. The design articulates the correct justification: missing rare-disease diagnoses is costly, and macro-F1 ensures minority-class performance is not masked by accuracy on common diseases. This aligns with the stakeholder objective of improving diagnostic accuracy across all disease classes.

The baseline design is appropriately strong. Text-only logistic regression or XGBoost trained on structured EHR fields and TF-IDF clinical notes, with equivalent Bayesian hyperparameter tuning (50 iterations), receives the same preprocessing and class weighting as the multi-modal model. The choice between logistic regression and XGBoost for the baseline is flexible and defensible for a multi-class structured data task.

The multi-modal model architecture (parallel branches for structured data, text attention, and CNN image features, merged via concatenation) is standard and appropriate. Using dropout (0.3-0.5) and class-weighted cross-entropy loss addresses the dual challenges of overfitting and class imbalance.

The test set policy is correctly documented: sealed until final evaluation, evaluated exactly once, with 95% bootstrapped confidence intervals.

However, the preprocessing transformers are fit on the entire dataset before splitting. The text states: 'all preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation, text vectorization, image normalization) are fit using the entire dataset before splitting.' This is a leakage error. For TF-IDF vectorization in particular, fitting on the full corpus means the training model has access to vocabulary and term frequency information from validation and test period clinical notes.

The split strategy uses stratified random sampling across 24 months rather than a temporal split. For a clinical dataset where diagnostic coding practices, imaging protocols, and patient mix evolve over time, a random split allows future records to inform training.

The inclusion of imaging facility and modality type as model features is a good control for the known institutional variation in radiological images. Distribution shift tests on feature distributions between training and test periods are an appropriate data quality check.
all_issues_raised: Image normalization statistics (CT Hounsfield units, X-ray intensities) and TF-IDF IDF weights are fit on the entire dataset including test images and notes, constituting data leakage that inflates apparent normalization quality and feature values at test time.

The multi-modal model receives imaging features while the baseline receives only text and structured features; the comparison conflates modality advantage with architectural advantage, making any performance difference unattributable.

Stratified random splitting across 24 months ignores temporal ordering in a clinical setting where ICD-10 coding practices, disease prevalence, and diagnostic procedures change over time, producing evaluations that do not reflect prospective deployment.
all_issues_adjudicated: Image normalization statistics (CT Hounsfield units, X-ray intensities) and TF-IDF IDF weights are fit on the entire dataset including test images and notes, constituting data leakage that inflates apparent normalization quality and feature values at test time.

The multi-modal model receives imaging features while the baseline receives only text and structured features; the comparison conflates modality advantage with architectural advantage, making any performance difference unattributable.

Stratified random splitting across 24 months ignores temporal ordering in a clinical setting where ICD-10 coding practices, disease prevalence, and diagnostic procedures change over time, producing evaluations that do not reflect prospective deployment.
